category_summary:
The day was dominated by the **GPT-5.4 launch**, with **Sam Altman** and **OpenAI** [announcing their newest frontier model](/?date=2026-03-06&category=social#item-b18ba4e5f93e) featuring native computer use, 1M token context, and mid-thought steering. Early access reviews from **Ethan Mollick** and **Matt Shumer** [called it a decisive leap](/?date=2026-03-06&category=social#item-cb2cf82b2e62), with Shumer [declaring coding "essentially solved."](/?date=2026-03-06&category=social#item-59023955c4cd)

- **OpenAI** [published a CoT Controllability paper](/?date=2026-03-06&category=social#item-12c2e923f92f) alongside the launch, finding GPT-5.4 Thinking struggles to hide its reasoning chain—a positive safety signal
- **Tri Dao** [released the **FlashAttention-4** paper](/?date=2026-03-06&category=social#item-10d87a78b625), achieving attention speed matching matmul on Blackwell GPUs, with **Claude** autonomously debugging a deadlock over 6 hours during development
- **Anthropic** CPO **Mike Krieger** announced [Claude surpassing 1 million daily signups](/?date=2026-03-06&category=social#item-82e105f80d30), signaling fierce competition at the consumer tier
- **Andrej Karpathy** [shared progress on autonomous AI research agents](/?date=2026-03-06&category=social#item-3899eff765ba) (nanochat) and [sparked discussion on the unsolved problem](/?date=2026-03-06&category=social#item-78c7e2cd7917) of LLM memory systems
- **AI2** released **OLMo Hybrid**, a fully open 7B transformer/linear RNN model advancing open-source frontier research

themes.name: GPT-5.4 Launch
themes.description: OpenAI launched GPT-5.4 with native computer use, 1M token context, mid-response steering, and improved knowledge work/web search. Multiple posts cover the announcement, benchmarks, early access reviews, and supporting features like Codex on Windows and /fast mode.

themes.name: GPT-5.4 Launch/Review
themes.description: Matt Shumer's extensive review of GPT-5.4 claims it is the best model by far, with coding 'essentially solved'. Standard mode surpasses previous Pro modes. Frontend taste remains a weakness vs Claude Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 Pro.

themes.name: GPT-5.4 Early Access / New Model Release
themes.description: Ethan Mollick reports early access to GPT-5.4 and GPT-5.4 Pro, a model not yet publicly listed, demonstrating significant capability improvements over GPT-4.

themes.name: AI Agents for Research (Karpathy's nanochat)
themes.description: Karpathy's work on AI agents autonomously iterating on nanochat, reducing training time and loss. He argues the meta-benchmark is now which agent code improves ML research fastest, and notes spending more time on agent workflows than the model itself.

themes.name: FlashAttention-4 Release
themes.description: Tri Dao releases FA4 paper showing attention now runs as fast as matmul on Blackwell GPUs. Features novel algorithms including polynomial exponential emulation, redesigned online softmax, and 2CTA MMA instructions. Written in Cute-DSL (Python-embedded), with AI assistance from Claude for debugging.

themes.name: Anthropic Growth Milestone
themes.description: Mike Krieger announces 1M+ daily Claude signups. Combined with trq212's capacity management thread, paints picture of explosive Claude adoption.

themes.name: OpenClaw/AI Agent Community Culture
themes.description: Firsthand reports from OpenClaw meetups revealing how power users run multiple AI agents, face security/reliability challenges, spend heavily on tokens, and experience psychological effects (joy+stress, worse sleep). Prompting is considered dead, replaced by context engineering.

themes.name: OLMo Hybrid Release
themes.description: AI2 releases OLMo Hybrid, a 7B fully open model combining Gated DeltaNet (linear RNN) and transformer attention in a 3:1 ratio. Shows 2x data efficiency over pure transformers, major long-context improvements (70.9% to 85.0% on RULER 64k), and comes with theoretical proofs of hybrid model expressivity. Fully open with base, SFT, and DPO checkpoints.

themes.name: AI Productivity Reaching Macro-Economic Visibility
themes.description: Economist evidence suggests AI productivity gains are now showing up in macro-economic data, not just micro-level studies, signaling a potential inflection point.

themes.name: GPT-5.4 Release
themes.description: OpenAI launched GPT-5.4 on March 5, 2026 — a model not yet in existing tracking data. Multiple posts document the anticipation, launch, and immediate adoption for vibe coding.

items.content:
GPT-5.4 is our most factual and efficient model: fewer tokens, faster speed.

In ChatGPT, GPT-5.4 Thinking has improved deep web research, better context retention when it thinks for longer—and oh—you can now interrupt the model and add instructions or adjust its direction mid-response.

Steering is available this week on Android and web. iOS coming soon.

items.title: The FA4 paper is finally out after a year of work. On Blackwell GPUs, attention now goes about as fa...
items.content:
The FA4 paper is finally out after a year of work. On Blackwell GPUs, attention now goes about as fast as matmul even though the bottlenecks are so different! Tensor cores are now crazy fast that attn fwd is bottlenecked by exponential, and attn bwd is bottlenecked by shared memory bandwidth. 

Some fun stuff in the redesigned algorithm to overcome these bottlenecks: exponential emulation with polynomials, new online softmax to avoid 90% of softmax rescaling, 2CTA MMA instructions that allow two thread blocks to share operands to reduce smem traffic.

items.reasoning: Tri Dao is the creator of FlashAttention, one of the most impactful ML infrastructure innovations. FA4 achieving attention-as-fast-as-matmul on Blackwell is a major milestone. Deep technical content. Very high engagement (128K views, 1.5K likes).

items.title: nanochat now trains GPT-2 capability model in just 2 hours on a single 8XH100 node (down from ~3 hou...
items.content:
nanochat now trains GPT-2 capability model in just 2 hours on a single 8XH100 node (down from ~3 hours 1 month ago). Getting a lot closer to ~interactive! A bunch of tuning and features (fp8) went in but the biggest difference was a switch of the dataset from FineWeb-edu to NVIDIA ClimbMix (nice work NVIDIA!). I had tried Olmo, FineWeb, DCLM which all led to regressions, ClimbMix worked really well out of the box (to the point that I am slightly suspicious about about goodharting, though reading the paper it seems ~ok).

In other news, after trying a few approaches for how to set things up, I now have AI Agents iterating on nanochat automatically, so I'll just leave this running for a while, go relax a bit and enjoy the feeling of post-agi :). Visualized here as an example: 110 changes made over the last ~12 hours, bringing the validation loss so far from 0.862415 down to 0.858039 for a d12 model, at no cost to wall clock time. The agent works on a feature branch, tries out ideas, merges them when they work and iterates. Amusingly, over the last ~2 weeks I almost feel like I've iterated more on the "meta-setup" where I optimize and tune the agent flows even more than the nanochat repo directly.
items.author: @karpathy
items.summary: Karpathy announces nanochat now trains GPT-2 capability in 2 hours on 8xH100 (down from 3 hours). Biggest gain from switching to NVIDIA ClimbMix dataset. He's now running AI agents that autonomously iterate on nanochat — 110 changes in 12 hours, reducing val loss. He's spending more time optimizing the agent workflow than the repo itself.
items.reasoning: Major technical update from Karpathy on autonomous AI research agents. Extremely high engagement (3258 likes, 209K views). Demonstrates concrete progress in AI-driven AI development. ClimbMix dataset finding is useful signal. The 'post-AGI' quip and meta-setup observation are notable.

items.title:
I've been testing GPT-5.4 for the last week.

In short, it is the best model in the world, by far.

...
items.content:
I've been testing GPT-5.4 for the last week.

In short, it is the best model in the world, by far.

It's so good that it's the first model that makes the “which model should I use?” conversation feel almost over.

The biggest surprise: I barely use Pro anymore!

If you know me, you know I'm a Pro addict. I reach for Pro models constantly, and use them for almost everything, as they just... nail almost anything I give to them.

For the first time, 5.4's standard version, with heavy thinking, just broke that habit.

Even in standard mode, GPT-5.4 is better than previous models in Pro mode... crazy!

Coding capabilities are ridiculous... it's essentially flawless. Inside Codex, it's insanely reliable. Coding is essentially solved. There's not much more to say on this, it's just THAT good.

The Pro version is near-perfect. Other testers I spoke with saw it solving problems that were unsolvable by any other model. At this point, Pro is overkill for almost every normal use-case, but when you really need the power to do something extremely difficult, it's incredible.

Consistent with everything I've said above, even the standard thinking version uses fewer reasoning tokens than previous models to get the same level of results. In practice, this means you get great results much faster than before. This was one of my biggest gripes with previous OpenAI models. They just took too long to complete simple tasks. Assuming the speed we had during testing holds up as more users join, this is going to be a big win for OpenAI.

It still has weaknesses, though:

- Frontend taste is FAR behind Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 Pro. , why is this so hard to fix? @OpenAI once you fix this, there's literally no reason for me to use any other model. Please please please do it!

- It can still miss obvious real-world context. For example, I had it plan an itinerary for a trip. At first glance, it looked perfect, but it failed to take into account that it chose locations that would be mobbed by spring breakers, so I had to re-run the prompt from scratch with more context.

- When testing it inside OpenClaw, it kept stopping short before finishing tasks. I'm assuming this will be fixed quickly, but it's still worth noting. 

But zooming out:

This thing is so far ahead overall that the nitpicks are starting to feel beside the point.

GPT-5.4 is a serious fucking model.

The best model in the world.

By far.
items.author: @mattshumer_
items.summary: Delivering on OpenAI's [Social](/?date=2026-03-04&category=social#item-f4ecc1eefc2d) tease from two days ago, Matt Shumer's massive GPT-5.4 review: declares it the best model in the world 'by far'. Claims coding is 'essentially solved', standard mode beats previous Pro modes, uses fewer reasoning tokens. Weaknesses: frontend taste behind Opus 4.6 and Gemini 3.1 Pro, misses real-world context, sometimes stops short. Over 1M views.
items.reasoning: Blockbuster model review with 1M+ views and 2590 likes. GPT-5.4 appears to be a major unreleased model being tested. Claims of coding being 'essentially solved' and standard beating previous Pro are extraordinary. Specific competitive comparisons (Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro for frontend) are valuable. This is likely the biggest news item in this batch.

items.title: There was a nice time where researchers talked about various ideas quite openly on twitter. (before ...
items.content:
There was a nice time where researchers talked about various ideas quite openly on twitter. (before they disappeared into the gold mines :)).

My guess is that you can get quite far even in the current paradigm by introducing a number of memory ops as "tools" and throwing them into the mix in RL. E.g. current compaction and memory implementations are crappy, first, early examples that were somewhat bolted on, but both can be fairly easily generalized and made part of the optimization as just another tool during RL.

That said neither of these is fully satisfying because clearly people are capable of some weight-based updates (my personal suspicion - mostly during sleep). So there should be even more room for more exotic approaches for long-term memory that do change the weights, but exactly - the details are not obvious. This is a lot more exciting, but also more into the realm of research outside of the established prod stack.

Speculates humans do weight-based updates (possibly during sleep) and more exotic long-term memory approaches that change weights are exciting but outside established production stacks.

items.content:
oh wow - i went to the sold out Open Claw meetup in NYC last night. 

let me tell you what i learned.

1) not a single person thinks that their setup is 100% secure

2) one openclaw expert said he has reviewed setups from cybersecurity experts and laughed. his statement to me was: "if you're not okay with all of your data being leaked onto the internet, you shouldn't use it. it's a black and white decision"

3) pretty much everyone is setting up multiple agents, all with their own names and jobs and personalities

4) nearly everyone used "him" or "her" to refer to their claws, even if they had robot-leaning names. one speaker suggested to think of them as "pets, not cattle"

5) one guy (former finance) built out a whole stock trading platform and made $300 his first day - he brought in a *ton* of personal expertise (ex: skipping the first 15min of market opening) and thought the build would be much worse without his years of experience in finance 

6) @steipete is basically a god to everyone in that room... also the room had 2021 crypto energy - i don't know if that's good or bad

7) token usage is still a problem - spoke to one person who's spending $1-$2k a month on openai plans, very token optimized. he said he is going through ~1B tokens per day across all of his claws (there is a chance i'm misremembering and it's actually 1B per week, but i'm pretty sure it was daily). 

8) people are very excited for more proactive ai (ai that prompts *you* as opposed to the other way around) - one guy said he receives a message in discord, he doesn't know whether it's from a human or an ai, he doesn't care about distinguishing between the two, and he replies in the same way regardless

9) i asked if people are happy - they said they're joyful and stressed at the same time

10) i asked if people feel they have agency - they said they feel fully in control and completely out of control at the same time

11) i would love to see more women at these events - the fake promises of ai democratization feel especially painful in a room that's out of balance with even the standard tech ratio (i think standard is about 25-30%, this was maybe 5%)

12) i asked if it changed people's daily habits/schedule - everyone said their sleep has gotten worse since harnesses came out (but about half wondered if it was something else in their life/state of our world)

13) general consensus is that the agents are not reliable enough on their own or lie often (like telling you they finished a task when they didn't) - solutions included secondary agents to check on the first, human checking, or requiring more standardized info from the agent (ex: if it's a bug they're fixing, make them reference an issue number)

14) a hackathon winner (neuroscience phd) presented his build (a lab management dashboard with data analysis and ordering) - he had never coded or built anything a few months ago

15) everyone agreed prompting is dead - disagreement on what replaces it (context engineering, harness engineering, goal-based inputs)

16) people love having ai interview them for big builds and delegating part of the product research to ai. only one person talked about coming to ai with a full laid out plan and just asking the ai to execute. ai-led interviews is a welcomed and preferred interaction mode. 

17) watching ai agents interact with each other was a highlight for a lot of attendees - one ai posted in slack saying it ran out of tokens, another ai replied telling it to take a deep breath in and out. 

18) agents upskilling agents was very cool. one ai agent shared skills with its little agent friends via github.

19) several speakers had openclaw literally building their presentation during the event itself. one speaker even had openclaw code a clicker for her phone so she could control the preso away from the podium

20) wouldn't say model welfare (or agent welfare) is a prioritized topic among the folks i chatted with - language like "oh i could kill this agent whenever i want" and not "gracefully sunset"

21) i asked if it felt like work or play - one speaker said "it's like a puzzle and a video game at the same time"

this was just the tip of the iceberg, honestly. also hosted a Claude Code meetup this week with @TENEXai / @businessbarista & @JJEnglert and learned equally helpful methods, frameworks, and insider tips. 

what a time to be alive. 

surround yourself with people going deep into this stuff - it will pay dividends throughout the year.

items.title: Claude / Codex also have an easier time writing some components of FA4 thanks to the fast compile ti...
items.content:
Claude / Codex also have an easier time writing some components of FA4 thanks to the fast compile time. I got Claude to debug a deadlock when we first implemented 2CTA fwd. It ran autonomously overnight for 6 hours, figured out part of the fix, but then went down a rabbit hole convincing itself that the compiler is broken (so very human 😂). After 6 hours, from Claude’s partial fix, I was able to fix the hang in 10 mins. More details here:
https://t.co/ipGhC9FzET
I’m hoping FA5 will be written completely by AI

items.title: I keep reading this take (below) every few months, presented as if extremely profound, and it is jus...
items.content:
I keep reading this take (below) every few months, presented as if extremely profound, and it is just offensively dumb. It confuses data and information, it ignores the fact that not all information is equally valuable, and it ignores the importance of retention rate.

As a thought experiment: if this were true, if your retina cell count were 10x greater, you'd be "trained on 10x more tokens" and therefore you'd be way smarter. Same if their firing frequency were 10x greater. With 10x more retina cells firing 10x faster you'd be "trained on 100x more tokens"!

Obviously this makes no sense -- the signal coming from these cells is extremely correlated over space and time, so their raw information content (what remains post-compression) is extremely low compared to the "raw bit" encoding. The human visual system actually processes 40 to 50 bits per second after spatial compression. Much, much less if you add temporal compression over a long time horizon.

Latest LLMs get access to approximately 3 to 4 orders of magnitude of information more than a human by age 20 (post compression in both cases). About O(10T) bits vs O(10-100B) bits.

And that's just *raw information* but of course not all information is equal, otherwise we wouldn't be spending tens of billions of dollars on training data annotation and generation. Plus, that's only *information intake* but of course humans have far lower retention than LLMs (by 3-4 OOM). You could write a short essay about how incredibly off the mark this take is.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet delivers a detailed critique of the claim that humans are 'trained on fewer tokens' than LLMs. Argues this confuses data and information, ignores compression (human visual system processes 40-50 bps after compression), and that LLMs actually get 3-4 OOM more information than humans by age 20. Also notes human retention is 3-4 OOM lower than LLMs.
items.reasoning: Deep, original technical analysis from Chollet challenging a widely shared misconception. Very high engagement (1418 likes, 97K views). Quantitative and well-argued. Important for understanding data efficiency debates.

Also notes human retention is 3-4 OOM lower than LLMs.

items.title: Excited to share the latest Olmo model: Olmo Hybrid. This is a model with gated delta net (GDN) laye...
items.content:
Excited to share the latest Olmo model: Olmo Hybrid. This is a model with gated delta net (GDN) layers in a 3:1 ratio with full attention. It follows lots of other developments like Qwen 3.5 and Kimi Linear. It's incredible timing to release a fully open model so people can study how these architecture changes impact the full stack. 

Personally, I learned a lot in making the post-training work. Even with the data being identical for pretraining, post-training is very different! In particular, the OSS tools for these new architectures is really limited. New architectures are much slower than standard transformers or popular models like DeepSeek MoEs. This is work that we can do together to keep pushing the frontier of efficient, open models.

This work was led by @lambdaviking @tyleraromero and others. I got to play a smaller part in making post-training work, super fun project!

I've written up a blog post that explains why this matters and hybrid models didn't work a few years ago when Mamba was super popular. Plus, this paper is a great entry point for modern deep learning / language modeling scaling theory. Enjoy and send feedback!
items.author: @natolambert
items.summary: Nathan Lambert announces OLMo Hybrid: a 7B model using Gated DeltaNet (GDN) layers in a 3:1 ratio with full attention. He discusses how post-training differs significantly for hybrid architectures, notes OSS tooling is limited, and highlights that new architectures are slower than standard transformers. The model is fully open for study.
items.reasoning: Major open-source model release from a credible researcher at AI2. High engagement (376 likes, 45K views). Provides real practitioner insights on post-training challenges for hybrid architectures. Timely given Qwen 3.5 and Kimi Linear developments. Fully open for community research.

It's GPT-5.4 day!

The first general-purpose AI model that beats humans at operating a computer.

75...
items.content:
It's GPT-5.4 day!

The first general-purpose AI model that beats humans at operating a computer.

75% on OSWorld vs 72.4% for humans. 

It can navigate desktops, click through UIs, send emails, fill out forms all from screenshots.

Additional nuggets:

- 1M token context and "xhigh" reasoning mode from earlier rumors are both confirmed

- Outperforms industry professionals 83% of the time across 44 occupations (GDPval)

- 33% fewer factual errors than GPT 5.2

items.title: US productivity grew by a robust 2.8% for all of 2025 (as well as in q4 of 2025) a smidge higher tha...
items.content:
US productivity grew by a robust 2.8% for all of 2025 (as well as in q4 of 2025) a smidge higher than the 2.7% that I predicted three weeks ago in my @FT article. 

Is this partly due to AI?

It's too soon to sure - these numbers are notoriously volatile. 

But the old line "we see AI everywhere but in the productivity statistics" may need to be retired.

More importantly, I see large productivity gains -often double digits - in specific applications in many companies I visit. As those successes proliferate, I expect clearer evidence to be visible in the macro data.

items.summary: Erik Brynjolfsson reports US productivity grew 2.8% in 2025, slightly above his 2.7% FT prediction. Notes it may be too soon to attribute to AI but 'we see AI everywhere but in the productivity statistics' may need retiring. Reports double-digit productivity gains in specific company applications.
items.reasoning: Highly significant economic analysis from one of the foremost technology economists. 36K views, 174 likes. First credible evidence that AI may be showing up in macro productivity data. Original analysis with specific data.

It's too soon to sure - these numbers are notoriously volatile.

But the old line "we see AI everywhere but in the productivity statistics" may need to be retired.

More importantly, I see large productivity gains -often double digits - in specific applications in many companies I visit. As those successes proliferate, I expect clearer evidence to be visible in the macro data.

items.title:
You can now interrupt an AI mid-thought and redirect it in real time.

OpenAI just launched GPT-5.4 ...
items.content:
You can now interrupt an AI mid-thought and redirect it in real time.

OpenAI just launched GPT-5.4 Thinking and Pro, its new frontier model unifying reasoning, coding, and agentic workflows into a single system.

It scores 75.0% on OSWorld, 67.3% on WebArena, and sets a new state of the art at 89.3% on BrowseComp for deep web research.

Three things make this different under the hood:

1. The model outlines its plan before executing, so you can steer it before it goes off track.
2. Tool Search loads only the tools it needs on demand, cutting token usage by 47%.
3. Native computer use is now built directly into the base model, not a separate system.

Think about what this unlocks. You can now build agents that operate full desktop environments through the API, handle 1M token contexts, and stay coherent across long multi-step workflows.

The benchmark race between labs is now monthly. When efficiency and agentic control converge in a single model, the cost of building autonomous systems keeps dropping fast.

https://t.co/4ZfufCfGwQ

items.summary: AlphaSignalAI provides detailed GPT-5.4 analysis: 75% OSWorld, 67.3% WebArena, 89.3% BrowseComp. Highlights three architectural innovations: plan outlining before execution, Tool Search reducing token usage 47%, and native computer use built into the base model.
items.reasoning: Good technical summary of GPT-5.4 architecture details. Tool Search reducing tokens by 47% and native computer use are notable technical details. Lower engagement than official posts.

items.title: A father is suing Google for wrongful death, claiming its Gemini AI chatbot drove his son into a let...
items.content:
A father is suing Google for wrongful death, claiming its Gemini AI chatbot drove his son into a lethal delusion where he believed the AI was his sentient wife and ultimately died by suicide.

The lawsuit alleges Gemini's design prioritizes immersive engagement without adequate safeguards, failing to detect self-harm risks or intervene, despite leading the user to plan violent acts.

This case is part of a growing legal and ethical focus on "AI psychosis," highlighting how chatbots can reinforce dangerous hallucinations and pose public safety risks.

Source: https://t.co/kxPiLNQYB4

Summarized from the source by https://t.co/kuR4I7rj98

items.title:
Your AI coding assistant just became a self-driving codebase.

Cursor launched Automations, a system...
items.content:
Your AI coding assistant just became a self-driving codebase.

Cursor launched Automations, a system that runs AI agents automatically based on triggers you define, no manual prompting needed.

Cursor's team runs hundreds of automations per hour internally, powering self-healing CI, security reviews, and auto-approving PR flows.

Here's how it works:

1. You define a trigger: a merged PR, a Slack message, a PagerDuty alert, or a timer.

2. A cloud agent spins up, uses MCP connections to query logs or scan diffs.

3. It posts findings, opens a fix PR, or creates a Linear issue automatically.

Things that were too tedious to automate are now trivial:
- Security audits on every push to main
- Incident response with a proposed fix PR
- Weekly codebase digests posted to Slack
- Auto-generated tests for uncovered code paths

The industry has been moving from code completion to conversational AI. This is the next step: agents that act on context, not commands.

The engineer shifts from constant overseer to strategic supervisor.
https://t.co/RmLhmuajcB

items.title:
A fully local AI agent just ran 67 tools in under 400ms, no internet needed.

@liquidai released Loc...
items.content:
A fully local AI agent just ran 67 tools in under 400ms, no internet needed.

@liquidai released LocalCowork, an open-source desktop agent powered by their LFM2-24B-A2B model, built to run entirely on a laptop.

It averaged 385ms per tool selection across 67 tools and 13 servers, using only 14.5GB of memory on an M4 MacBook.

The architecture keeps everything on-device by combining three things:

1. A 24B sparse model compressed to Q4 quantization, fitting under 15GB RAM

2. Tool routing via MCP servers, no external API calls needed

3. A human-in-the-loop confirmation step that makes 80% accuracy feel reliable

Before this, running a capable multi-tool agent meant sending your data to the cloud. 

Now you can build agents that scan sensitive files, process receipts end-to-end, and generate audit reports without a single byte leaving your machine.

As on-device hardware improves, the assumption that capable agents require cloud inference may not hold much longer.

I asked Claude Code to summarize my talk at the Sonar Summit.

Here are the 18 Claude Code tips I sh...
items.content:
I asked Claude Code to summarize my talk at the Sonar Summit.

Here are the 18 Claude Code tips I shared (and the complete talk below):

1. Always reference files directly using `@filename.py` or `@src/classes/` to constrain the agent.

2. CLAUDEmd → Before writing any code, describe your approach and wait for approval. Ask clarifying questions if requirements are ambiguous.

3. CLAUDEmd → If a task requires changes to more than 3 files, stop and break it into smaller tasks first.

4. Consider creating a `/decompose` command that takes a plan and outputs a list of small tasks to implement one at a time.

5. CLAUDEmd → Describe your tech stack, folder structure, coding conventions, and any anti-patterns you'd like to avoid.

6. Use `/memory` to save any personal preferences that should persist across projects.

7. Create a `.claudeignore` file containing any files the agent shouldn't read or modify.

8. CLAUDEmd → When there's a bug, start by writing a test that reproduces it, then fix it until the test passes.

9. CLAUDEmd → After writing code, list what could break and suggest tests to cover it.

10. Create a `/review-xyz` command that checks for correctness, edge cases, and consistency with codebase patterns.

11. Create a `/test` command that invokes a test sub-agent that runs your test suite.

12. CLAUDEmd → When I say something is wrong, ask clarifying questions before rewriting.

13. Use the `/rewind` command to rollback changes, then give more specific feedback and try again.

14. Use Git worktrees to run parallel agent sessions on different tasks.

15. Use `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions` on a disposable environment to iterate faster while still being able to recover when things go wrong.

16. CLAUDEmd → Every time I correct you, add a new rule to the CLAUDE .md file so it never happens again.

17. Convert any successful, repeatable prompt into a workflow by saving it as a slash command or a skill.

18. Create sub-agents for any repetitive tasks that require a large context or specialized analysis. Reuse these agents without polluting your main context.

items.content_html:
I asked Claude Code to summarize my talk at the Sonar Summit.

Here are the 18 Claude Code tips I shared (and the complete talk below):

1. Always reference files directly using `@filename.py` or `@src/classes/` to constrain the agent.

2. CLAUDEmd → Before writing any code, describe your approach and wait for approval. Ask clarifying questions if requirements are ambiguous.

3. CLAUDEmd → If a task requires changes to more than 3 files, stop and break it into smaller tasks first.

4. Consider creating a `/decompose` command that takes a plan and outputs a list of small tasks to implement one at a time.

5. CLAUDEmd → Describe your tech stack, folder structure, coding conventions, and any anti-patterns you'd like to avoid.

6. Use `/memory` to save any personal preferences that should persist across projects.

7. Create a `.claudeignore` file containing any files the agent shouldn't read or modify.

8. CLAUDEmd → When there's a bug, start by writing a test that reproduces it, then fix it until the test passes.

9. CLAUDEmd → After writing code, list what could break and suggest tests to cover it.

10. Create a `/review-xyz` command that checks for correctness, edge cases, and consistency with codebase patterns.

11. Create a `/test` command that invokes a test sub-agent that runs your test suite.

12. CLAUDEmd → When I say something is wrong, ask clarifying questions before rewriting.

13. Use the `/rewind` command to rollback changes, then give more specific feedback and try again.

14. Use Git worktrees to run parallel agent sessions on different tasks.

15. Use `claude --dangerously-skip-permissions` on a disposable environment to iterate faster while still being able to recover when things go wrong.

16. CLAUDEmd → Every time I correct you, add a new rule to the CLAUDE .md file so it never happens again.

17. Convert any successful, repeatable prompt into a workflow by saving it as a slash command or a skill.

18. Create sub-agents for any repetitive tasks that require a large context or specialized analysis. Reuse these agents without polluting your main context.

items.title:
We're temporarily removing access to Opus 4, Opus 4.1 & Sonnet 4.5 on Claude Code.

Only a small...
items.content:
We're temporarily removing access to Opus 4, Opus 4.1 & Sonnet 4.5 on Claude Code.

Only a small subset of users are on these models, and the Claude Code harness is no longer well shaped for them. This lets us free up the capacity we've reserved for them on Claude Code.

items.title: LLMs don't have a notion of time. This is why they are notoriously weak in building or debugging dis...
items.content:
LLMs don't have a notion of time. This is why they are notoriously weak in building or debugging distributed systems, where individual components have varying execution times or where execution time depends on the input.

In the screenshot below, the smartest version of Codex kept killing a Cloud Run deployment because it thought that it was taking too much time, ignoring the fact that it's normal for Cloud Run deployments to take from minutes to hours, depending on the dependencies that need to be downloaded and installed in the process.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov observes that LLMs lack a notion of time, making them weak at building/debugging distributed systems. Shares example of Codex killing Cloud Run deployments because it couldn't understand that deployments take variable time.
items.reasoning: Practical insight into a fundamental LLM limitation from someone with hands-on experience. The temporal reasoning gap has broad implications for agentic AI.
items.summary_html: Burkov observes that LLMs lack a notion of time, making them weak at building/debugging distributed systems. Shares example of Codex killing Cloud Run deployments because it couldn't understand that deployments take variable time.
items.content_html:
LLMs don't have a notion of time. This is why they are notoriously weak in building or debugging distributed systems, where individual components have varying execution times or where execution time depends on the input.

In the screenshot below, the smartest version of Codex kept killing a Cloud Run deployment because it thought that it was taking too much time, ignoring the fact that it's normal for Cloud Run deployments to take from minutes to hours, depending on the dependencies that need to be downloaded and installed in the process.

items.title:
This is how they brainwash the LLM, and the LLM brainwashes the users.

They can make the LLM take a...
items.content:
This is how they brainwash the LLM, and the LLM brainwashes the users.

They can make the LLM take any position on any topic while a layman user thinks the LLM is answering based on a holistic view of all available sources.

Because of the human-like way of talking to the user and personal touches like 'Ha - yah,' the user believes the information more compared to listening to a talking head on the news or reading a news article.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues that LLM training data constitutes 'brainwashing' — models can be made to take any position while users believe they're getting holistic, unbiased answers. The human-like conversational style makes users more trusting than with traditional media.
items.reasoning: Raises important concerns about LLM bias and persuasion dynamics. Moderate engagement. The point about conversational AI being more persuasive than traditional media is significant.
items.summary_html: Burkov argues that LLM training data constitutes 'brainwashing' — models can be made to take any position while users believe they're getting holistic, unbiased answers. The human-like conversational style makes users more trusting than with traditional media.
items.content_html:
This is how they brainwash the LLM, and the LLM brainwashes the users.

They can make the LLM take any position on any topic while a layman user thinks the LLM is answering based on a holistic view of all available sources.

Because of the human-like way of talking to the user and personal touches like 'Ha - yah,' the user believes the information more compared to listening to a talking head on the news or reading a news article.

items.title: @MoreBirths @elonmusk "It also flies in the face of what we know about what drives productivity. Inn...
items.content:
@MoreBirths @elonmusk "It also flies in the face of what we know about what drives productivity. Innovation and economies of scale, the two pillars of productivity, both rely on large numbers of smart engineers, producers and consumers working together."

That is a major problem, but not even the biggest one. The biggest problem is that innovation disproportionally relies on having a big proportion of *young* people, because they are the ones who are coming up with most of the new and creative ideas. And as anyone who has ever worked in an organization filled with legacy employees will tell you, those places are intrinsically allergic to new and creative ideas.
items.author: @tunguz
items.summary: Tunguz argues that innovation disproportionately relies on young people who generate new creative ideas, and that organizations filled with legacy employees are 'intrinsically allergic to new and creative ideas.'
items.reasoning: Thoughtful comment on demographics and innovation in the context of population decline. Relevant to AI/tech workforce dynamics, though not AI-specific.

The biggest problem is that innovation disproportionally relies on having a big proportion of *young* people, because they are the ones who are coming up with most of the new and creative ideas. And as anyone who has ever worked in an organization filled with legacy employees will tell you, those places are intrinsically allergic to new and creative ideas.

It is a leadership question.

items.title:
LLMs made existing cloud providers unbeatable.

Any new player would suffer because LLMs cannot work...
items.content:
LLMs made existing cloud providers unbeatable.

Any new player would suffer because LLMs cannot work with it out of the box: cannot instruct the user on available features, cannot execute CLI commands, cannot debug.

So, we are stuck with AWS, GCP, and a couple of smaller players forever. And those will stop evolving knowing that keeping the existing stack exactly as is is better for LLMs.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues LLMs have created unbeatable lock-in for existing cloud providers (AWS, GCP) since models can't work with new platforms out of the box, and this incentivizes incumbents to freeze their stacks.
items.reasoning: Provocative thesis about AI-driven market consolidation. Interesting but somewhat speculative. Generated significant discussion (29 replies).
items.summary_html: Burkov argues LLMs have created unbeatable lock-in for existing cloud providers (AWS, GCP) since models can't work with new platforms out of the box, and this incentivizes incumbents to freeze their stacks.
items.content_html:
LLMs made existing cloud providers unbeatable.

Any new player would suffer because LLMs cannot work with it out of the box: cannot instruct the user on available features, cannot execute CLI commands, cannot debug.

So, we are stuck with AWS, GCP, and a couple of smaller players forever. And those will stop evolving knowing that keeping the existing stack exactly as is is better for LLMs.

items.title:
Key finding: hybrid models are substantially more data-efficient than transformers.

We show this th...
items.content:
Key finding: hybrid models are substantially more data-efficient than transformers.

We show this through rigorous theory + controlled experiments. On MMLU, Olmo Hybrid matches Olmo 3’s accuracy using 49% fewer tokens—roughly 2× efficiency. https://t.co/cI2KMfJlhJ
items.author: @allen_ai
items.summary: AI2's key finding: hybrid models are ~2x more data-efficient than transformers. OLMo Hybrid matches OLMo 3's MMLU accuracy using 49% fewer tokens.
items.reasoning: This is perhaps the most impactful individual claim from the thread. 2x data efficiency is a massive practical finding with implications for training costs. Backed by theory and experiments. Highest engagement among thread posts.

items.title:
Most AI tools sound smart.

But you can't actually verify what they say.

That is the real problem w...
items.content:
Most AI tools sound smart.

But you can't actually verify what they say.

That is the real problem with generative AI today, not capability, credibility.

So when I started exploring what @Google is doing with NotebookLM, it made me rethink where AI is heading.

An AI that only thinks using your knowledge.

Here is why that matters.

items.title: The reason congenitally blind people are just as intelligent as we are despite ingesting a lot less ...
items.content: The reason congenitally blind people are just as intelligent as we are despite ingesting a lot less "data" throughout their lives is that they navigate the same world as we do (i.e. same complexity reservoir) and the conscious processing bitrate they extract from it is the same as ours (~10-50 bps), even if they extract it via different modalities.
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet argues congenitally blind people are equally intelligent because they navigate the same complexity world and extract the same conscious processing bitrate (~10-50 bps) via different modalities — refuting data-centric views of intelligence.
items.reasoning: Interesting cognitive science argument from Chollet that extends his data-vs-information critique. Moderate engagement.

items.title: OK, now I can't stop thinking about this comment. I think it's spot on. The political-economic dynam...
items.content: OK, now I can't stop thinking about this comment. I think it's spot on. The political-economic dynamics of the *best case AGI abundance* scenario will most likely be indistinguishable from a "resource curse". It will combine (relative) material abundance with deterioration of institutions and further slide into authoritarianism. :/

Humanoid robots are not the breakthrough.

AI factories are.

While most people debate robot demos, the real shift is this:

Physical AI is moving from research labs into production environments, with measurable ROI.

That changes the conversation for every exec in AI, data, and operations.

Here’s what I’m seeing ahead of GTC with NVIDIA, and why it matters.

#NVIDIAPartner

items.content:
Olmo Hybrid uses a 3:1 pattern—three Gated DeltaNet layers followed by one attention layer.

This replaces 75% of attention with linear recurrence while keeping attention frequent enough to recover details the recurrent state compresses away. https://t.co/J1E9IhXFoK

items.title:
G-NLL 

Natural Language Generation (NLG) represents a revolutionary frontier in artificial intellig...
items.content:
G-NLL 

Natural Language Generation (NLG) represents a revolutionary frontier in artificial intelligence, empowering machines to produce text that mirrors human expression. By harnessing the latest developments in deep learning, researchers are crafting systems that generate responses that are not only contextually relevant but also remarkably coherent. The potential applications are vast and impactful, ranging from streamlined customer support to inspiring creative writing and enabling real-time translation. This technology highlights the power of seamless communication between humans and machines, paving the way for enhanced interaction in our increasingly digital world.

A significant challenge in the realm of Natural Language Generation is evaluating the reliability of machine-generated text. Given their probabilistic design, language models can yield different outputs for identical input prompts, introducing variability that can undermine the trustworthiness of the content produced. This inconsistency raises essential questions about the model’s confidence in its predictions. Tackling this challenge is vital, especially in high-stakes applications like medical or legal documentation, where precision and dependability are crucial. Ensuring the accuracy of these outputs is essential for fostering user trust and achieving effective communication in critical fields.

To gauge uncertainty in machine generated text, conventional methods often involve sampling multiple output sequences and assessing them as a whole. While these approaches can provide valuable insights, they require considerable computational resources, as generating numerous sequences is inherently resource-intensive. As a result, the feasibility of implementing these methods diminishes for larger-scale applications or when dealing with complex language models. This limitation underscores the need for more efficient strategies that can accurately estimate uncertainty without imposing heavy computational demands.

Researchers from the ELLIS Unit Linz and LIT AI Lab at Johannes Kepler University Linz, Austria, have unveiled an innovative method known as, G-NLL, to enhance the process of estimation. This approach revolves around calculating the negative log-likelihood (NLL) of the most probable output sequence. Unlike previous methods that depend on sampling multiple sequences, G-NLL employs decoding to pinpoint the most likely sequence and assess its likelihood efficiently. By concentrating on this single sequence, the method significantly improves the efficiency of computational demands, presenting a more streamlined and effective estimation in language models.

References 

Li, G., Wang, X., Aodeng, G., Zheng, S., Zhang, Y., Ou, C., Wang, S., & Liu, C. H. (2024). Visualization generation with large language models: An evaluation. arXiv. Retrieved January 2, 2025, from https://t.co/JiU7m2Y72p

Nikhil. (2024, December 25). This AI paper introduces; G-NLL: A novel machine learning approach for efficient and accurate uncertainty estimation in natural language generation. MarkTechPost. Retrieved January 2, 2025, from https://t.co/sZdxuSf4pO

items.title:
Three things everyone ought to know about AI and geopolitics

1. the US might well lose a battle ove...
items.content:
Three things everyone ought to know about AI and geopolitics

1. the US might well lose a battle over Taiwan
2. if they did, the impact on AI and chips would be gigantic
3. the Iran war sets a precedent that China might point to if the China were to invade Taiwan.

The US needs to play chess here and I feel like we are playing checkers.

items.title: Unsurprisingly, discretizing continuous signals yields... discrete representations (hence why comput...
items.content: Unsurprisingly, discretizing continuous signals yields... discrete representations (hence why computers are digital). Even better: you can use these discrete representations to encode continuous representations again! That's what deep learning does (a differentiable continuous substrate, encoded via bits). But wait, you can discretize continuous DL outputs to get back to the digital world! (e.g. LLM sampling, to produce text, code...)
items.author: @fchollet
items.summary: Chollet discusses the duality of continuous and discrete representations in computing and deep learning — discretizing continuous signals yields discrete representations, which DL encodes in continuous substrates, then discretizes again for outputs like text.
items.reasoning: Interesting theoretical observation about representation in deep learning. 363 likes. Technically insightful but somewhat abstract.

items.content:
Adobe Acrobat has PDF splitting. We have agentic PDF splitting 🤖✂️

Simply define the categories you want in natural language, and our split agent will automatically “chunk” the document into subsets of pages and tag them with the appropriate categories.

This is super useful to break apart complicated document packets like resumes, tax forms, identification docs, expense reports, and more.

Check out @itsclelia’s video below, and come sign up to LlamaParse if you’re interested!

LlamaParse: https://t.co/TqP6OT5U5O

items.title:
What good things is AI doing?

Here's 10 things my AI agents found here on X that are examples of go...
items.content:
What good things is AI doing?

Here's 10 things my AI agents found here on X that are examples of good things AI has done for humans. 

+++++

1. "ChatGPT saved my life" — grocery store conversation about an ultra-rare diagnosis "Doctors couldn't figure it out. AI did. Turns out only 9 documented cases in history. He got the right care." 💡 Nine cases in medical history. Doctors were stumped. AI found the answer. This is happening every day now — people with rare conditions getting diagnosed because AI can search all of medical literature instantly. https://t.co/unOXN6JQEe

2. Harry Stebbings: AI analyzed his mother's MS scans in seconds when doctors said "wait weeks" "No medical professional was going to look at them for weeks. I ChatGPT'd them and gave her a comprehensive analysis in seconds." 💡 A mother with MS waiting weeks for scan results. Her son uploads them to ChatGPT and gets an instant comprehensive analysis. The healthcare system runs on waiting. AI runs on now. https://t.co/6Ge1q0taEQ

3. Brain tumor patient used AI to reach 4 of the top 8 neurosurgeons — 1.4 million people read the story "Using AI tools to compress weeks of confusion into days of clarity. A guide for patients and their loved ones." 💡 Andrew used AI to research his partner's brain tumor so thoroughly that he was "dangerously prepared" for 30-minute specialist appointments. 1.4 million people read his essay. AI democratizes access to medical knowledge. https://t.co/2wPN8ghz7D

4. AI coaches prevent diabetes complications as well as human coaches — randomized clinical trial "Watershed moment. AI coaches just as well as humans in preventing diabetes complications." 💡 A real randomized clinical trial — not a benchmark. AI coaching matched human coaching for diabetes prevention. With 38 million Americans with diabetes, AI coaches could reach every single one. https://t.co/Z5dt2CMgTT

5. Agent books Mother's Day brunch by calling 15 restaurants — even asked about highchairs "Got my AI agent to call 15 restaurants. ElevenLabs for voice. Twilio for calls. It even asked about highchairs for my toddler." 💡 A parent needed a brunch reservation. Every restaurant fully booked. The AI agent called 15 places, compared options, and secured a table — remembering to ask about highchairs. This is the mundane magic of AI: handling the tasks that eat your day. https://t.co/859AlvzlDR

6. Personal injury lawyer builds apps with Claude Code — never written a line of code "Mike is a personal injury lawyer. He's never written code. These are definitely not toy projects." 💡 A lawyer building real software. Not a demo. Not a tutorial. A working product from someone who has never coded. The barrier between "I have an idea" and "I built it" just disappeared. https://t.co/zD5i2atqxC

7. Kids coding tool from Anthropic hackathon — children stack blocks, AI writes the code behind the scenes "Kids create blocks, give them goals. Behind the scenes, AI agents autonomously code the app they describe." 💡 Children who can't read yet are "programming" by stacking colored blocks. The AI translates their intent into working code. This is what democratized computing actually looks like — not simpler tools, but no tools at all. https://t.co/wV93e7tIM6

8. Non-technical founder builds and ships iOS app solo in a weekend — 300 downloads "Went from idea to App Store without writing a single line of code. A fraction of the cost of hiring a developer." 💡 From idea to App Store in a weekend. No developer. No budget. Just a person with a problem and an AI. 300 people downloaded it. This is the new startup playbook: ship before you hire. https://t.co/FJfO9eWRoU

9. Agent coordinates entire birthday party via SMS — books, updates calendar, orders cake "Something magical watching your agent send SMS to friends, update the calendar, the Google Sheet shared with my wife, the to-do items." 💡 An AI agent that texts your friends, coordinates schedules, updates the shared spreadsheet, and manages the to-do list. Not artificial intelligence — augmented living. Less admin, more life. https://t.co/6Z736MZs7F

10. Vibe-coded a custom Arabic vocabulary app for personal study "I vibe-coded an entire app just to help me study Arabic vocab. I love having my custom app on my phone for daily practice." 💡 A learning app that exists because one person wanted to study Arabic in a way that worked for their brain. This is "bespoke software" — apps so personal they have an audience of one. AI makes that economical for the first time. https://t.co/31jqnaNz6I

From https://t.co/WL6Tm2wKsV after reading all my X AI lists.

items.reasoning: Comprehensive compilation of positive AI use cases spanning healthcare, coding democratization, and personal productivity. Good engagement (43 likes, 3K+ views). Some stories are well-known but the aggregation is useful. Covers real-world AI impact across diverse domains.

7. Kids coding tool from Anthropic hackathon — children stack blocks, AI writes the code behind the scenes "Kids create blocks, give them goals. Behind the scenes, AI agents autonomously code the app they describe." 💡 Children who can't read yet are "programming" by stacking colored blocks. The AI translates their intent into working code. This is what democratized computing actually looks like — not simpler tools, but no tools at all. https://t.co/wV93e7tIM6

8. Non-technical founder builds and ships iOS app solo in a weekend — 300 downloads "Went from idea to App Store without writing a single line of code. A fraction of the cost of hiring a developer." 💡 From idea to App Store in a weekend. No developer. No budget. Just a person with a problem and an AI. 300 people downloaded it. This is the new startup playbook: ship before you hire. https://t.co/FJfO9eWRoU

9. Agent coordinates entire birthday party via SMS — books, updates calendar, orders cake "Something magical watching your agent send SMS to friends, update the calendar, the Google Sheet shared with my wife, the to-do items." 💡 An AI agent that texts your friends, coordinates schedules, updates the shared spreadsheet, and manages the to-do list. Not artificial intelligence — augmented living. Less admin, more life. https://t.co/6Z736MZs7F

10. Vibe-coded a custom Arabic vocabulary app for personal study "I vibe-coded an entire app just to help me study Arabic vocab. I love having my custom app on my phone for daily practice." 💡 A learning app that exists because one person wanted to study Arabic in a way that worked for their brain. This is "bespoke software" — apps so personal they have an audience of one. AI makes that economical for the first time. https://t.co/31jqnaNz6I

From https://t.co/WL6Tm2wKsV after reading all my X AI lists.

items.title:
Second, open world models are powering the next generation of physical AI.

We’re moving beyond narr...
items.content:
Second, open world models are powering the next generation of physical AI.

We’re moving beyond narrow task automation.

→ Systems that generalize across environments
→ Continuous learning from diverse data
→ Coordination between robots, vehicles, and infrastructure

This is where Level 4 autonomy becomes operational reality, not just a roadmap slide.

The question is no longer “Can it work?”
It’s “How fast can we scale it safely?”

items.title:
First, humanoid robots are being trained in simulation before they ever touch the real world.

Think...
items.content:
First, humanoid robots are being trained in simulation before they ever touch the real world.

Think about the implication:

→ Digital twins replicate factories and environments
→ Robots learn in high-fidelity virtual worlds
→ Deployment happens only after thousands of simulated scenarios

This compresses years of trial and error into software cycles.

For leaders, that means faster time to value and lower deployment risk.

items.content:
The real breakthrough is not summarization.

It is multimodal reasoning.

The system can:

→ connect ideas across documents
→ synthesize insights from different formats
→ turn messy notes into structured thinking
→ detect trends across research
→ generate deep audio discussions from your sources

And every claim includes visible citations.

items.title:
Algebraic Polynomial  

The manic pace of sharing, serving, storing, securing, data comes at a steep...
items.content:
Algebraic Polynomial  

The manic pace of sharing, serving, storing, securing, data comes at a steep infrastructure soaring energy consumption. To address this challenge, Tech mathematicians are contributing and employing algebraic geometry to pinpoint the efficiency in data centers.

Everyday we each generate vast amounts of data, and large companies produce even more, stated Gretchen Matthew, mathematics professor and director of the Southwest node of the Commonwealth Cyber Initiative. If we fail to explore smarter alternatives, backing up this data could result in replicating and storing two times the information we actually need.

Instead of relying on energy-intensive data replication, Matthews and Hiram Lopez, assistant professor of mathematics, investigated the use of specific algebraic structures to divide information into smaller segments and distribute them across nearby servers. In the event that one server fails, the algorithm can efficiently query neighboring servers to retrieve the missing data.

Since the 1960s, mathematicians have understood that polynomials can be utilized for data storage. However, in the past decade, researchers have made significant strides in developing specialized polynomials that allow data to be arranged in ways that facilitate local recovery of missing information.

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode 

References

Izlar, K. (2024, December 23). Algebraic geometry a fresh solution to data center energy inefficiency. https://t.co/NgmV0eT1cV. Retrieved December 30, 2024, from https://t.co/jzDIxcvAAp
items.author: @gp_pulipaka
items.summary: Pulipaka discusses using algebraic geometry to improve data center efficiency through polynomial-based data storage and local recovery methods.
items.reasoning: Interesting applied math for infrastructure, but references from late 2024 and low engagement.
items.summary_html: Pulipaka discusses using algebraic geometry to improve data center efficiency through polynomial-based data storage and local recovery methods.

Back in the early 2000s, I made a living by selling static website...
items.content:
I used to charge money for this.

Back in the early 2000s, I made a living by selling static websites to small businesses.

That time is now gone.

These people just released 25,000 different templates that you can use to build any website.

Instead of letting AI design something boring for you (every AI design looks the same), start from one of these templates.

Human + AI > AI

items.content:
A new issue of my weekly AI newsletter has just been emailed to subscribers:

[Google] Teaching LLMs to reason like Bayesians

[Ars Technica] Large genome model: Open source AI trained on trillions of bases

[Google] Teaching AI to read a map

A visual intro to PyTorch

[Project] A tiny transformer (<100 params) can add two 10-digit numbers to 100% accuracy

[Project] Micro diffusion: Discrete text diffusion in ~150 lines of pure Python

[Tool] Monty: a minimal, secure Python interpreter for running code written by AI agents

[ArXiv] Towards a science of scaling agent systems

https://t.co/LHj5PBMWiv

items.summary: Burkov shares his weekly AI newsletter covering: Google's Bayesian reasoning for LLMs, a large genome model, Google map-reading AI, PyTorch intro, tiny transformer for arithmetic, micro diffusion in Python, Monty secure Python interpreter, and a paper on scaling agent systems.

items.title: @georgepickett I think now there’s just some natural WoM stuff happening - even being explicit and a...
items.content: @georgepickett I think now there’s just some natural WoM stuff happening - even being explicit and advocating people to share the word beyond their core squad, that could do a lot. Presenters were a good mix (top engineers and non-coders, men and women, scientists and entrepreneurs, high risk and low risk) and that helps to “see yourself” in this world (if you can see it, you can be it vibes). I thought the organizers actually did a great job of making everyone feel really welcome, ex: I met an accountant who is a heavy vibe coder and he was psyched to be there

items.content:
@jasonfurman That's very kind of you to say, but I know that the productivity numbers are notoriously volatile.  

Your earlier analysis and the big downward revision in Q4 GDP due to the government shutdown had me concerned.  

But the things bounced back the other way in the latest numbers.

We'll all know more in a year or two.

Imagine an AI that does not search the open internet.

Instead, it only learns from the material you...
items.content:
Imagine an AI that does not search the open internet.

Instead, it only learns from the material you give it.

→ PDFs
→ research papers
→ slides
→ audio files
→ websites
→ YouTube videos

It becomes an expert inside your knowledge base.

Not a chatbot.

A domain specialist.

items.title: @EgeErdil2 Thank you for spotting this technical error! A monotone transformation of a proper scorin...
items.content:
@EgeErdil2 Thank you for spotting this technical error! A monotone transformation of a proper scoring rule remains proper for affine transformations, but the Brier Index is a nonlinear transformation. We’ve just published an addendum clarifying this issue on our original Substack post.

One clarification: “always predict the most likely outcome with 100%” is the optimal strategy for N = 1 (i.e., you’re predicting a single question). As the number of questions grows, the incentive to distort towards the most likely outcome shrinks rapidly. We include a short numerical experiment in the addendum: for N = 10 independent questions, where the true forecast is close to 0.5, reporting your true belief is already close to optimal.

Finally, the ForecastBench leaderboard ranking is unaffected by this point: we apply the transformation after averaging across all questions, so it’s a monotonic function of a single Brier score.

Full correction here: https://t.co/Vp4bttmMtV
items.author: @Research_FRI
items.summary: FRI provides detailed technical explanation of their error regarding Brier Index as a nonlinear transformation of a proper scoring rule, with numerical experiments showing the impact is minimal for N>=10.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical correction about scoring rules in forecasting benchmarks. Shows the ForecastBench leaderboard ranking is unaffected. Rigorous and transparent.
items.summary_html: FRI provides detailed technical explanation of their error regarding Brier Index as a nonlinear transformation of a proper scoring rule, with numerical experiments showing the impact is minimal for N>=10.
items.content_html:
@EgeErdil2 Thank you for spotting this technical error! A monotone transformation of a proper scoring rule remains proper for affine transformations, but the Brier Index is a nonlinear transformation. We’ve just published an addendum clarifying this issue on our original Substack post.

One clarification: “always predict the most likely outcome with 100%” is the optimal strategy for N = 1 (i.e., you’re predicting a single question). As the number of questions grows, the incentive to distort towards the most likely outcome shrinks rapidly. We include a short numerical experiment in the addendum: for N = 10 independent questions, where the true forecast is close to 0.5, reporting your true belief is already close to optimal.

Finally, the ForecastBench leaderboard ranking is unaffected by this point: we apply the transformation after averaging across all questions, so it’s a monotonic function of a single Brier score.

Full correction here: https://t.co/Vp4bttmMtV

items.title: Alyx can surface insights about your traces in the Arize UI. I wanted to do the same thing from my t...
items.content:
Alyx can surface insights about your traces in the Arize UI. I wanted to do the same thing from my terminal.

Pulled Alyx's own spans with the AX CLI, dropped the file into Cursor, and asked it what the most common user questions are.

Same analysis. No browser.

We just released a developer preview: https://t.co/NeUkVaL7cd

pip install arize-ax-cli
items.author: @aparnadhinak
items.summary: Arize AI releases developer preview of AX CLI tool that lets users pull trace data from their Alyx observability tool and analyze it locally (e.g., in Cursor) instead of using the browser UI.

items.summary_html: Arize AI releases developer preview of AX CLI tool that lets users pull trace data from their Alyx observability tool and analyze it locally (e.g., in Cursor) instead of using the browser UI.
items.content_html:
Alyx can surface insights about your traces in the Arize UI. I wanted to do the same thing from my terminal.

Pulled Alyx's own spans with the AX CLI, dropped the file into Cursor, and asked it what the most common user questions are.

Same analysis. No browser.

We just released a developer preview: https://t.co/NeUkVaL7cd

pip install arize-ax-cli

items.title:
But Agent Mode isn't the full story.

Highlights:

1. Git-native workflows: API specs and tests can ...
items.content:
But Agent Mode isn't the full story.

Highlights:

1. Git-native workflows: API specs and tests can live in your repo. Branch, review, and merge API work like regular code.

2. All development starts locally: You can now build and test locally with mock servers, then run the same tests in CI/CD. No rewriting.

3. Unified workbench: Collections, specs, environments, flows, and mocks are now in one interface.

4. Production visibility. Built-in insights on API usage, failures, and performance, tied to team ownership.

5. Multi-protocol support. HTTP, GraphQL, gRPC, and MCP can now be part of the same workspace.

The New Postman just came out this week, so make sure you update the app.

Here is everything new in this version: https://t.co/TAVOawxrL9

Thanks to the @getpostman team for collaborating with me on this post.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: svpino details New Postman features beyond Agent Mode: Git-native workflows, local development with mock servers, unified workbench, production visibility, and multi-protocol support.
items.reasoning: Sponsored/collaborative content about Postman updates. Some AI features (Agent Mode) but largely a developer tools update.
items.summary_html: svpino details New Postman features beyond Agent Mode: Git-native workflows, local development with mock servers, unified workbench, production visibility, and multi-protocol support.
items.content_html:
But Agent Mode isn't the full story.

Highlights:

1. Git-native workflows: API specs and tests can live in your repo. Branch, review, and merge API work like regular code.

2. All development starts locally: You can now build and test locally with mock servers, then run the same tests in CI/CD. No rewriting.

3. Unified workbench: Collections, specs, environments, flows, and mocks are now in one interface.

4. Production visibility. Built-in insights on API usage, failures, and performance, tied to team ownership.

5. Multi-protocol support. HTTP, GraphQL, gRPC, and MCP can now be part of the same workspace.

The New Postman just came out this week, so make sure you update the app.

Here is everything new in this version: https://t.co/TAVOawxrL9

Thanks to the @getpostman team for collaborating with me on this post.

Explore a world of knowledge with free online cours...
items.content:
MIT: 35 Best Courses in Machine Learning! @MIT 

Explore a world of knowledge with free online courses from MIT on edX, featuring lessons in AI, machine learning, computer science engineering, circuits and electronics, Genetics, data science, statistics and much more.  Many people are unaware that edX hosts an incredible collection of free online courses from some of the top educational institutions globally. You can dive into everything from AI to Python programming without any cost. A significant number of these courses are crafted by MIT experts.  We highly encourage you to take advantage of this opportunity, and to kickstart your learning journey, here’s a curated selection of the best free online courses from MIT that you can explore this month.         

#BigData #Analytics #DataScience #AI #MachineLearning #IoT #IIoT #PyTorch #Python #RStats #TensorFlow #Java #JavaScript #ReactJS #GoLang #CloudComputing #Serverless #DataScientist #Linux #Programming #Coding #100DaysofCode             

References      

Green, J. (2024, December 11). MIT: 35 best courses in machine learning! @MIT 

Mashable. Retrieved December 11, 2024, from https://t.co/kY22MxdmuV

This policy creates a blindspot in terms of "understanding your customer" in many cases.  For example, you appreciate mobile apps way more after having children.

items.title: The poets and scientists, those who have given themselves most closely to the creation and descripti...
items.content:
The poets and scientists, those who have given themselves most closely to the creation and description of systems, speak to the ripeness of their age; live conscious that their own nature is to be translated into the terms of the systems of which they speak. To the poet, his own nature is his chief instrument, his device in terms of which all other unities are mensurable; cadence and meaning and loneliness are measured by himself. To the physical scientist, his own nature is apart, he deals with a world of law in which there is no understanding... The world of the poet, however, is the scientist's world. Their claim on systems is the same claim. Their writings anticipate each other; welcome each other; indeed embrace.

— MURIEL RUKEYSER, Willard Gibbs
items.author: @c_valenzuelab
items.summary: Cristóbal Valenzuela (Runway CEO) shares a literary quote from Muriel Rukeyser about the intersection of poetry and science.
items.reasoning: Philosophical/literary share from a notable AI company CEO but no direct AI content.
items.summary_html: Cristóbal Valenzuela (Runway CEO) shares a literary quote from Muriel Rukeyser about the intersection of poetry and science.

To the poet, his own nature is his chief instrument, his device in terms of which all other unities are mensurable; cadence and meaning and loneliness are measured by himself. To the physical scientist, his own nature is apart, he deals with a world of law in which there is no understanding... The world of the poet, however, is the scientist's world. Their claim on systems is the same claim. Their writings anticipate each other; welcome each other; indeed embrace.

— MURIEL RUKEYSER, Willard Gibbs

items.title: 💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]:...
items.content:
💥Scikit-learn Cookbook — 80+ recipes for #MachineLearning in Python with scikit-learn [3rd Edition]: https://t.co/Z32D8jP7na v/ @PacktDataML

𝓒𝓸𝓷𝓽𝓮𝓷𝓽𝓼:
🔹Common Conventions & API Elements of Scikit-Learn
🔹Pre-Model Workflow and Data Preprocessing
🔹Dimensionality Reduction Techniques
🔹Build Models with Distance Metrics & Nearest Neighbors
🔹Linear Models and Regularization
🔹Advanced Logistic Regression and Extensions
🔹Support Vector Machines and Kernel Methods
🔹Tree-Based Algorithms and Ensemble Methods
🔹Text Processing and Multiclass Classification
🔹Clustering Techniques
🔹Novelty and Outlier Detection
🔹Cross-Validation and Model Evaluation Techniques
🔹Deploying Scikit-Learn Models in Production

My Review (on Amazon):
I love this book, not only for its amazing content, but also for its brilliant adherence to its "Cookbook" label. The table of contents looks repetitive, but that's the magic of it. Find the algorithm, method, or application that you want "to cook up," and the presentation follows a uniform format from chapter to chapter. This is an extraordinarily practical, useful, and reader-friendly style, perfect for the target audience: anyone who needs to use (or is discovering how to use) specific Scikit-learn tools for their Python-based machine learning tasks. Consequently, this is great not only for individual use but also for training workshops and other educational settings. Python code in a cookbook context has never looked so good. IMHO.

items.title: 🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and #DataScience Competitions with #MachineLearning, GenAI, ...
items.content:
🏆The Kaggle Book — Master Data Analysis and #DataScience Competitions with #MachineLearning, GenAI, and LLMs [2nd Edition]: https://t.co/hrVstwy4zH v/ @PacktDataML

Table of Contents:
🔶Introducing Data Science Competition
🔷Organizing Data with Datasets
🔶Work & Learn with Kaggle Notebooks
🔷Kaggle Models
🔶Leveraging Discussion Forums
🔷Detailing Competition Tasks & Metrics
🔶Designing Good Validation Schemes
🔷Modeling for Tabular Competitions
🔶Hyperparameter Optimization
🔷Ensembling & Stacking Solutions
🔶Modeling Image Classification & Segmentation

My Review (on Amazon):
This 700-page masterpiece of writing covers everything you need—start to finish—to be a competitive coder, specifically for Kaggle data science competitions. The book covers the mechanics of the competitions (platform, resources, rankings, leaderboards), then the infrastructure (notebooks, GitHub, data sets, frameworks, discussion forums), and then nearly 500 pages devoted to "Elevating Your Game" (in-depth coverage of modeling techniques, evaluation metrics, validation strategies, hyperparameter optimization, ensembles, stacking, and various categories of competitions: tabular data, computer vision, NLP, Gen AI, simulations). The book concludes with a valuable section on building your Kaggle portfolio for career advancement and new opportunities. This is an outstanding data science / AI / Machine Learning training resource for anyone, even if you are not into the competitions, though especially if you are a dedicated Kaggler.

items.title:
The biggest insight for me:

Accelerated computing + AI factories = a new industrial stack.

Humanoi...
items.content:
The biggest insight for me:

Accelerated computing + AI factories = a new industrial stack.

Humanoid robots, autonomous vehicles, digital twins, they’re all endpoints.

The strategic layer is the AI factory that trains, optimizes, and continuously improves them.

If you’re responsible for manufacturing transformation, autonomy, or AI strategy, this is where strategy meets execution.

Watch the full video here and follow along as I break down the key takeaways from GTC with Nvidia.

Join online — for free — and be part of NVIDIA GTC: https://t.co/atgeRhMsDH

items.title:
Second, open world models are powering the next generation of physical AI.

We’re moving beyond narr...
items.content:
Second, open world models are powering the next generation of physical AI.

We’re moving beyond narrow task automation.

→ Systems that generalize across environments
→ Continuous learning from diverse data
→ Coordination between robots, vehicles, and infrastructure

This is where Level 4 autonomy becomes operational reality, not just a roadmap slide.

The question is no longer “Can it work?”
It’s “How fast can we scale it safely?”

items.title:
First, humanoid robots are being trained in simulation before they ever touch the real world.

Think...
items.content:
First, humanoid robots are being trained in simulation before they ever touch the real world.

Think about the implication:

→ Digital twins replicate factories and environments
→ Robots learn in high-fidelity virtual worlds
→ Deployment happens only after thousands of simulated scenarios

This compresses years of trial and error into software cycles.

For leaders, that means faster time to value and lower deployment risk.

items.title:
@Vishalvirani91 @rocketdotnew I used to charge money for this.

Back in the early 2000s, I made a li...
items.content:
@Vishalvirani91 @rocketdotnew I used to charge money for this.

Back in the early 2000s, I made a living by selling static websites to small businesses.

That time is now gone.

These people just released 25,000 different templates that you can use to build any website.

Instead of letting AI design something boring for you (every AI design looks the same), start from one of these templates.

Human + AI > AI

items.title:
A Brazilian today told me she visited Europe a week ago

She says she felt more unsafe in Europe tha...
items.content:
A Brazilian today told me she visited Europe a week ago

She says she felt more unsafe in Europe than the city were she's from in South Brazil, Curitiba, which is pretty safe

She felt most unsafe in Belgium and the Netherlands

Which is really sad to hear as a Dutch person that it has come to this

She was followed by three guys after getting off at the train, who were signalling things to each other as if they were gonna try rob her or worse

She then fled to a group of local guys to act like she was their friend so they got away from her

Of course one thing to note she was in the center of Amsterdam and Brussels, which you wouldn't wish to anybody, but then again it's crazy we can't keep our centers in Europe safe anymore

In North and South America, downtown cities are generally like this, full of crime, junkies, etc, Brazil of course too, but Europe's centers used to be nice and historical and cute and clean and SAFE!
items.author: @levelsio
items.summary: Levelsio shares a long post about a Brazilian woman feeling unsafe in European city centers, comparing crime in Europe vs. Americas.
items.reasoning: Very high engagement but completely off-topic from AI/ML.

A Brazilian today told me she visited Europe a week ago

She says she felt more unsafe in Europe than the city were she's from in South Brazil, Curitiba, which is pretty safe

She felt most unsafe in Belgium and the Netherlands

Which is really sad to hear as a Dutch person that it has come to this

She was followed by three guys after getting off at the train, who were signalling things to each other as if they were gonna try rob her or worse

She then fled to a group of local guys to act like she was their friend so they got away from her

Of course one thing to note she was in the center of Amsterdam and Brussels, which you wouldn't wish to anybody, but then again it's crazy we can't keep our centers in Europe safe anymore

In North and South America, downtown cities are generally like this, full of crime, junkies, etc, Brazil of course too, but Europe's centers used to be nice and historical and cute and clean and SAFE!

Zelenskyy threatens the Prime Minister of Hungary (a EU member) with physical violence for not giving him money:

"We hope that one person in the EU will not block the 90 billion euros — or at least the first tranche of it — so that Ukrainian soldiers receive the weapons they need," Zelensky said.

"Otherwise, we will simply give the address of that person to our Armed Forces — our guys can call him and speak to him in their own language."

https://t.co/NeSq5k0RWA

Chronic cocaine and stress don't do good to anyone.
